mei.description: Two people sharing the same star lodge are like mirrors of each other, deeply understanding one another's thoughts and feelings. This strong resonance brings powerful connection, but it also means both strengths and weaknesses get amplified.
mei.detailed: Mei (命) means two people share the same natal star lodge. In Sukuyodo, this is like being twins. Because you share the same stellar energy, your thinking patterns, values, and even behavioral habits are remarkably similar. This similarity creates an unspoken understanding between you. However, precisely because you're so alike, the flaws you see in the other person are often the same issues you need to face in yourself. The key to growth in this relationship is: accepting the other person means accepting yourself. Through your interactions, you can recognize and improve your own shortcomings.
mei.advice: The strengths and challenges of this relationship are equally clear. Try to appreciate your similarities, and treat the shared weaknesses you discover as opportunities for growth.
tips: Accepting their flaws is learning to accept your own
Set growth goals together to avoid stagnating as a pair
Give each other alone time to prevent becoming too enmeshed
Treat them as a mirror for self-reflection — watch how you react in the relationship
When friction arises, look inward first before discussing each other's feelings
avoid: Picking apart each other's flaws while ignoring your own self-reflection
Expecting them to change the things you're unwilling to face in yourself
Letting your similarity breed competition over who gets to take the lead
Reading their behavior as 'they're targeting me' when it's really your own shadow
Spending too much time together and losing space for independent growth
good_for: Mutual respect among competitors in the same field
mei.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Mei: when two share the same lodge, they are as reflections in a mirror — virtues and flaws alike. They are one body, sharing fortune and misfortune together.

gyotai.description: The deepest karmic bond in Sukuyodo. There's often an uncanny sense of familiarity when you meet. This connection goes beyond ordinary fate, carrying a destined quality. Regardless of how things unfold, you'll leave a lasting mark on each other's lives.
gyotai.detailed: Gyotai (業胎) is the most mystical relationship type in Sukuyodo. 'Gyo' (業) represents karmic ties accumulated from the past, and 'Tai' (胎) represents bonds that will carry into the future. When you encounter someone in a Gyotai relationship, you often feel like you've met them before — as if your souls already know each other. This relationship isn't always smooth — it can bring major life turning points or tests. Whether joyful or painful, people in a Gyotai bond play a significant role in your life, pushing you toward growth and transformation. This is a special connection worth treasuring.
gyotai.advice: For such a deep bond, let things unfold naturally and stay present with the experience. Don't chase it or run from it — just move through this relationship with awareness.
tips: Let the relationship develop naturally without forcing outcomes
See this bond as a chance to learn and grow
No matter what happens, go through it with gratitude
Stay open to the life changes they bring, even when the process is uncomfortable
Write down important insights from your time together — these are often pivotal turning points
avoid: Dismissing the significance of this special connection
Using 'destiny' as an excuse to dodge responsibility
Giving up on nurturing the relationship just because it feels 'fated'
Over-mystifying the bond while ignoring practical relationship issues
Using the Gyotai connection as leverage to guilt the other person into staying
good_for: Encounters that bring major turning points
gyotai.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Gyotai: deep karmic ties from past lives, bound by cause and effect. The bond was formed in the womb, destined from birth. Linked across three lifetimes, this is no chance encounter.

eishin.description: The most auspicious pairing in Sukuyodo. When together, both people elevate and empower each other. Whether in business or romance, this combination brings positive influence and is traditionally the most recommended match.
eishin.detailed: Eishin (栄親) is considered the ideal relationship type in Sukuyodo. 'Ei' (栄) means prosperity, and 'Shin' (親) means closeness. The hallmark of this relationship is that both people become better versions of themselves when together. You bring out their potential, they support your growth, creating a positive cycle. At work, this combination produces results greater than the sum of its parts. In love, you're the kind of couple that goes the distance and grows together. Traditional Sukuyodo considers this the best match for marriage because you continue to bring each other positive energy over the long term.
eishin.advice: This is a rare and wonderful connection. Actively nurture it. Appreciate the positive impact you have on each other, but don't get complacent just because things come easily.
tips: Collaborate actively — your combined efforts multiply results
Support each other and grow upward together
This relationship is worth long-term investment
Regularly express gratitude so they know you value the positive impact they bring
Set long-term goals together and pursue them — this bond strengthens through shared purpose
avoid: Taking their contributions for granted
Neglecting the relationship just because it's going well
Enjoying their support without giving equal effort in return
Skipping communication because things are smooth, assuming they'll always go along with you
Treating this good fortune like a safe deposit box that needs no maintenance
good_for: Any collaborative relationship worth investing in long-term
eishin.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Eishin: the most auspicious match. 'Ei' means flourishing, 'Shin' means harmony. When these two meet, they radiate light together — all things go smoothly, and blessings multiply.

near.description: Near-distance Eishin is the strongest auspicious match in Sukuyodo. Your energy fields resonate intensely, and from the first meeting you get that feeling of 'I've found them.' You naturally understand and support each other without any effort — being together feels as easy as breathing. What you do, they instinctively appreciate. Your flaws even seem charming in their eyes. The power of this bond is that you don't complement each other — you resonate. Two people on the same frequency together amplify energy instead of draining it.
near.love: Near-distance Eishin romance tends to develop fast and deep. The time from first spark to committed relationship is much shorter than usual because you barely need to guess — you read each other instantly. The biggest advantage is that 'security doesn't need to be manufactured.' You naturally trust them, and they naturally put you at ease. But because everything flows so smoothly, it's easy to start taking each other for granted. The most important lesson for the long term: even when maintaining the relationship requires no effort, keep expressing appreciation and care. Don't stop communicating just because there are no problems — saying 'you matter to me' once in a while makes an already great relationship even better.
near.career: In the workplace, your near-distance Eishin chemistry makes you highly efficient at pitching proposals and seeking promotions together. You're well-suited to present to leadership as a team and pursue key projects. When collaborating, define roles clearly since your skills and focus areas tend to overlap. Sync up regularly to turn your intuitive chemistry into concrete plans.
near.advice: Cherish this connection — it's the most blessed bond in Sukuyodo. Your natural chemistry is a gift, but maintaining the relationship still takes intention.
tips: Express gratitude regularly — don't let familiarity swallow appreciation
Create new shared experiences from time to time so the relationship doesn't plateau
When disagreements arise, communicate patiently — you're so used to being in sync that being out of step feels extra jarring
Keep some independent hobbies and friend circles to give the relationship breathing room
Set long-term goals together and check in periodically — channel your shared energy toward bigger things
avoid: Overlooking their emotional shifts just because things are going well
Demanding perfect alignment on everything — occasional differences are healthy
Crediting 'luck' for this relationship's quality instead of actively maintaining it
Retreating to your own corners under external pressure instead of facing it together
Treating each other as a given — every relationship needs tending

mid.description: Mid-distance Eishin has a 'slow and steady' kind of fortune. Unlike near-distance, you don't click instantly — your bond takes a bit of time to build, but once established it's remarkably solid. At first you might think they're nice but nothing special. As you spend more time together, you keep discovering more to appreciate — that 'grows on you' feeling. Your dynamic has a comfortable level of space: close enough to feel secure, but not so close that it's suffocating.
mid.love: Mid-distance Eishin romance takes the steady route. There won't be a movie-style lightning bolt, but there will be a warmth that gradually deepens. Your love is like a slow-simmered soup — it takes time, but the flavor ends up richer than a quick stir-fry. The advantage of this pacing is that you have room to get to know the real person without the illusion wearing off too quickly. You'll have minor friction along the way, but it's the kind where you understand each other better after the dust settles. Don't rush to define the relationship — let feelings develop naturally. The odds are strongly in your favor.
mid.career: Mid-distance Eishin collaboration works like a puzzle — you each bring perspectives the other lacks. Treat each other as long-term strategic advisors. When pursuing promotions, expanding business, or building networks, leverage each other's strengths. Before making big decisions, get their input — they'll often catch your blind spots.
mid.advice: Don't overlook this relationship just because the initial spark isn't intense. Time will prove this is a bond well worth investing in.
tips: Give the relationship enough time to develop — don't bail because the initial chemistry isn't electric
Create opportunities for deeper conversation — you'll discover each other's value through meaningful exchange
Appreciate the ways you complement each other — what you struggle with, they might handle effortlessly
Keep a consistent rhythm of interaction — you don't need daily contact, but don't let it fade out
Take on a challenge together — overcoming difficulties side by side accelerates your bond
avoid: Underestimating this relationship's potential just because there's no 'love at first sight'
Comparing it to flashier relationships — every pace has its strengths
Only reaching out when you need help
Overlooking their quiet contributions — mid-distance people tend to show affection subtly
Mistaking stability for boredom — this kind of stability is what many people long for

far.description: Far-distance Eishin has a 'talent scout meets hidden gem' quality. Your differences are bigger than near or mid-distance pairings, and at first meeting you might not feel anything special — you might even think you're completely different people. But fate keeps bringing you back together, and each encounter yields unexpected rewards. Their worldview, lifestyle, and way of thinking are all different from yours, but those differences are exactly the nourishment you need.
far.love: Far-distance Eishin romance feels like a serendipitous adventure — you probably met in an unexpected setting and come from very different backgrounds. But those differences create a unique attraction: they possess qualities you admire but don't have yourself. The relationship needs patience to develop, and early on you might clash over lifestyle habits or communication styles. But once you work through the adjustment period, you become irreplaceable to each other. This relationship teaches you that loving someone doesn't require them to be like you — it's about finding harmony within your differences.
far.career: Far-distance Eishin's cross-domain synergy makes you well-suited to pitch to decision-makers or develop new business together. What you're good at, they're not, and vice versa — together your proposals are more compelling than either alone. You're a great fit for projects or opportunities that require cross-disciplinary integration.
far.advice: Embracing differences is at the heart of this relationship. The distance between you keeps every encounter fresh — something near-distance bonds rarely experience.
tips: Approach their differences with curiosity rather than judgment
Share your world with them — invite them into your daily life and step into theirs
Don't try to change them — your differences are exactly what makes this bond valuable
Schedule regular deep conversations to avoid drifting apart due to busy lives
Document your shared experiences — looking back, you'll see that every encounter brought something meaningful
avoid: Giving up on communication because the gaps feel too wide — patience is essential here
Only interacting within your own comfort zone — venture into their world
Interpreting their differences as a lack of understanding or caring
Ignoring the small but important things you have in common — those are the bridges connecting you
Questioning the relationship's value at the first sign of friction — the adjustment process itself is growth

yusui.description: Being together feels easy and comfortable, like hanging out with an old friend you've known for years. But this comfort can lead both of you to settle into the status quo, lacking the drive to push each other forward. Great for casual friendships, but watch out for stagnation in more serious relationships.
yusui.detailed: Yusui (友衰) is defined by effortless, pressure-free interactions. 'Yu' (友) represents the friendly comfort, while 'Sui' (衰) hints at the potential for drain and decline. Together, there's no tension — you can fully relax and be yourself, which is genuinely precious. However, because it's so comfortable, you can easily settle into a shared comfort zone and lose the motivation to improve. This relationship works well as a life reset button — the perfect companion when you need to unwind. But if you're looking at this as a work partnership or long-term romance, pay close attention to whether both of you are still growing.
yusui.advice: While enjoying the comfort, consciously set shared goals to prevent the relationship from sliding into complacency. Check in periodically to make sure you're both still moving forward.
tips: Set shared growth goals and hold each other accountable
Enjoy the comfort without drowning in complacency
Regularly check whether the relationship is developing in a positive direction
Try doing something new together once in a while to inject fresh energy
Agree to share recent progress and learnings with each other at regular intervals
avoid: Sinking into the comfort zone together and enabling each other's laziness
Losing your drive to improve because things feel too easy
Using 'I don't want to pressure them' as an excuse to avoid growth
Defining the relationship as purely recreational and shutting down any serious conversations about the future
Thinking the relationship doesn't need real attention just because it's effortless
yusui.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Yusui: close and familiar like old friends. Yet too much ease breeds complacency, draining energy and fortune. Best sustained through mutual encouragement.

near.description: Near-distance Yusui is the kind of relationship where you don't even need to make plans — you just show up in each other's lives. You probably live close by, overlap in social circles, or met in the same environment. Being together is completely stress-free. They're like that worn-in old t-shirt — so comfortable you forget it's there. You can lounge around doing nothing all day without a hint of awkwardness, or just coexist in the same room doing your own things and feel perfectly content. The problem is that this comfort is dangerously easy to sink into.
near.love: Near-distance Yusui romance feels like a natural upgrade from friendship. You know each other so well that you might have skipped the confession entirely — somewhere along the line you just became a couple. The biggest strength of this relationship is authenticity — they've seen you at your worst, your most disheveled, and they still choose to be by your side. But the most common issue is 'too comfortable to have ambition.' You might fall into a routine of binge-watching shows, ordering takeout, and scrolling your phones side by side — cozy days that quietly let you both stagnate. Try committing to doing one thing you've never done together each month. It doesn't have to be a big adventure — a new restaurant, a new skill — just enough to add a healthy dose of novelty.
near.career: In the workplace, near-distance Yusui brings smooth interpersonal dynamics but isn't suited for bold moves. Focus on clearing your existing backlog rather than chasing new ventures or job changes. Accept the resources and development your organization offers, and use your easygoing chemistry to tackle accumulated tasks.
near.advice: This is a relationship that lets you recharge, but relaxation isn't the same as giving up on growth. Deliberately inject some challenges and goals to keep the bond both comfortable and nourishing.
tips: Commit to trying one new thing together each month to keep things fresh
Share what you're each working on and let their progress inspire you
Don't spend all your free time together — preserve your independent space
Have an honest conversation about future plans and dreams once in a while, not just everyday small talk
When you catch both of you slacking off, get moving together — even just a walk or a workout
avoid: Settling into the comfort zone together and telling yourselves 'this is fine'
Stopping personal growth because things are too comfortable, pouring all energy into leisure
Using 'relaxation' as an excuse to dodge problems and challenges that need facing
Making each other your entire social circle — you both need other people to bring different perspectives
Setting zero goals or expectations for the relationship and letting everything drift

mid.description: Mid-distance Yusui is the 'we're both busy, but hanging out is always a good time' kind of relationship. You're not attached at the hip, but every meetup is relaxed, fun, and filled with endless conversation. Your social circles overlap somewhat but aren't identical, which gives you fresh topics and perspectives to share when you get together. This moderate distance lets you enjoy the comfort without tipping into complacency.
mid.love: Mid-distance Yusui romance has a warm 'old friends turned lovers' quality. Your understanding of each other isn't built on guesswork but on years of observation and experience. After getting together, the biggest perk is that 'you rarely step on landmines' because you already know where they are. But because you know each other so well, you miss out on the thrill of the unknown. To keep the spark alive, create unknowns on a familiar foundation — visit somewhere new, try something you've never done, have a deep conversation on a topic you've never touched before.
mid.career: In the workplace, mid-distance Yusui is good for social gatherings but not for launching new ventures or job-hopping together. Focus on solving problems in your current roles and consult each other when challenges come up. Doing solid work with what you have beats opening new fronts.
mid.advice: Enjoy the ease but don't stop there. The comfort between you can become the foundation for facing challenges together.
tips: Maintain a regular but not excessive meetup frequency
Share one thing the other doesn't know about your life each time you meet
Do challenging activities together to break out of the comfort zone
Recommend your latest discoveries and favorites to each other
When they hit a rough patch, offer real help rather than just words of comfort
avoid: Only reaching out when you want to relax, turning the relationship into a pure escape hatch
Avoiding serious topics because hanging out is always so pleasant
Going silent for long stretches then suddenly reappearing — keep a steady rhythm
Only sharing the good stuff while hiding your struggles — this relationship can handle realness
Comparing the 'intensity' of your bond with other people's relationships

far.description: Far-distance Yusui has the curious chemistry of 'friends from different worlds.' Your backgrounds, lifestyles, and ways of thinking are quite different, but strangely, once you're together you can slip into 'chill mode' almost instantly. Their world is nothing like yours, and that actually makes hanging out more interesting — you're hearing a completely different version of a life story, and you find yourself understanding it perfectly.
far.love: Far-distance Yusui romance has a touch of 'cross-cultural love' to it — your life paths wouldn't normally cross, but some twist of fate brought you together. The differences keep things fresh and prevent boredom. But differences also bring real adjustment needs: different lifestyle habits, different social circles, maybe even different spending philosophies. The key to success is finding the core values you truly share — everything else is negotiable.
far.career: Far-distance Yusui brings smooth cross-domain communication, making you great sounding boards for each other's ideas. But don't invest heavily or start a business together. Stay focused on your own careers, handle your own challenges, and treat each other as stress-relief conversation partners rather than business partners.
far.advice: What makes this relationship special is that it combines comfort with novelty. Use your differences to create fun, and use your common ground to build trust.
tips: Actively explore and experience each other's world — this is the richest part of the relationship
Find the things you both care about despite all your differences, and anchor the relationship there
Accept that you'll never see eye to eye on some things — that doesn't affect your chemistry
Share funny stories from your respective worlds and laugh together
Far distance requires more deliberate maintenance — proactively schedule time together
avoid: Trying to pull them into your world without being willing to enter theirs
Letting differences breed a sense of superiority or inferiority
Only reaching out when you want to relax, forgetting they might need your support too
Defining them by stereotypes
Assuming the relationship won't last just because of distance or differences

ankai.description: A combination with intense attraction but also the most challenges. There's a clear power imbalance between the two — one plays the 'An' role (providing stability) while the other plays the 'Kai' role (bringing disruption). This relationship demands high self-awareness and deliberate care.
ankai.detailed: Ankai (安壊) is the most tension-filled relationship type in Sukuyodo. This pairing often sparks instant, powerful attraction that's hard to resist. Yet there's an inherent imbalance between the two: the 'An' side provides stability and support, while the 'Kai' side unintentionally causes harm or disruption to the 'An' side. This imbalance can show up in emotional investment, power dynamics, or psychological states. The 'Kai' side needs to recognize their influence and learn restraint. The 'An' side needs to establish healthy boundaries for self-protection. Handled well, this relationship brings profound growth. Handled poorly, both sides can get hurt.
ankai.advice: Enter this relationship with clear-eyed awareness. First, figure out whether you're the 'An' or the 'Kai,' then consciously adjust how you interact to prevent harm.
tips: Identify which role you play in the relationship
If you're the Kai side, consciously dial back your influence
If you're the An side, learn to protect yourself and set boundaries
Check in with each other regularly about how you're feeling — catch power imbalances early
If the relationship heads toward an extreme, don't hesitate to seek outside help or counseling
avoid: The Kai side exerting excessive control or causing unconscious harm
The An side becoming overly dependent or completely losing their sense of self
Ignoring obvious inequality in the relationship
Using 'I'm doing this for your own good' to rationalize controlling behavior
Ignoring warning signs and discomfort just because the attraction is overwhelmingly strong
good_for: Encounters that bring major life transformations
Relationships that demand high awareness and intentional care
Potentially intense short-term connections that require caution
ankai.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Ankai: the strongest attraction, yet fortune and misfortune are intertwined. The An receives, the Kai destroys. Proceed with care. With self-awareness and balance, disaster can be turned into blessing.

near.description: Near-distance Ankai is the most dangerously magnetic combination in Sukuyodo. You're like two magnets with a pull so strong it's nearly impossible to resist — the first time you meet, it feels like getting struck by lightning. When your eyes lock, the air itself seems to freeze. The intensity of this bond far exceeds normal human interaction, and both the good and the bad get amplified to the extreme. The An side gets deeply drawn to something ineffable about the Kai side and becomes willing to give beyond all rational limits. The Kai side may unconsciously keep taking, because it all feels so natural.
near.love: Near-distance Ankai love is a dangerous blaze. The chemistry between you is more intense than any other relationship type — passion hits hard, and conflict hits just as hard. The An side loves deeply and unconditionally, constantly sacrificing their own needs and boundaries for the Kai side. The Kai side enjoys being loved and may unknowingly slip into a dominant or even controlling position. The greatest risk is the An side gradually losing themselves. Before entering this relationship, honestly ask yourself: 'Am I in love with this person, or am I in love with the feeling of being drawn in?' If both sides can stay clear-headed and consciously address the power imbalance, this relationship can lead to profound growth. But if either side starts feeling more pain than happiness, seriously consider setting boundaries or seeking professional help.
near.career: In the workplace, the An side excels at creating stable environments and building systems, while the Kai side handles thorny problems and deals with competitors. When roles are clearly defined, efficiency is high — but the Kai side tends to overstep. The An side should establish their own channels for speaking up; written reports are safer than one-on-one conversations.
near.advice: This is the relationship in Sukuyodo that most requires conscious effort. Attraction doesn't equal compatibility, and passion doesn't equal love. Maintaining clear self-awareness is the survival rule for this bond.
tips: Clearly identify whether you're the An or Kai side, and adjust your behavior accordingly
An side: learn to say no to unreasonable demands — your boundaries shouldn't disappear for anyone
Kai side: examine whether you're unconsciously controlling or draining the other person
Set relationship health markers — if your emotional state has been unstable for over three months, it's time to reassess
Find a trusted third party (friend or therapist) to regularly and objectively review the state of your relationship
avoid: Treating the intense attraction as 'meant to be' and abandoning rational judgment
An side: continuously compromising and losing yourself to keep the relationship going
Kai side: assuming their devotion is a given without reflecting on your own impact
Staying in a relationship you know is unhealthy because you can't bear to leave
Dismissing well-meaning warnings from people around you — bystanders see what you might not

mid.description: Mid-distance Ankai comes with an extra layer of buffer compared to near-distance. The attraction is still there, but it won't make you lose your mind. The power imbalance is more subtle — not the obvious control-and-be-controlled dynamic, but rather nuanced differences in how you interact. The An side might feel they're putting in a bit more emotional effort. The Kai side might feel they're the one with more ideas and decisiveness. This imbalance won't cause obvious damage, but over time it can leave the An side feeling things aren't quite fair.
mid.love: Mid-distance Ankai romance falls somewhere between intense and calm. There's attraction but not to the point of losing control; there's friction but not to the point of tearing things apart. Sometimes your time together is sweet, sometimes it's mentally draining — mainly because your levels of investment and role positioning aren't quite equal. The good news is that mid-distance Ankai is the most likely combination to reach a healthy state through mutual effort. The key is that both sides are willing to face the imbalance head-on, then consciously work to adjust it.
mid.career: Mid-distance Ankai can form effective partnerships: the An side handles relocation, settling into new environments, and stability-oriented work, while the Kai side tackles difficult problems. Document the division of labor clearly to avoid the Kai side inadvertently overstepping when responsibilities overlap.
mid.advice: This relationship has real room for improvement, but both sides need to be self-aware. Regular honest conversations about how you're each feeling is the best way to maintain balance.
tips: Regularly share your feelings about the relationship — especially anything that feels unfair
An side: practice voicing your opinions and preferences even on small matters
Kai side: deliberately ask for their input on decisions and genuinely consider it
Set shared goals so it feels like you're fighting side by side rather than in a hierarchy
Address growing imbalance promptly through conversation instead of bottling it up until you explode
avoid: An side: using 'whatever you want' or 'up to you' to avoid expressing your real thoughts
Kai side: treating the An side's cooperation as the default you're entitled to
Letting resentment build until an emotional breakdown
Digging up old grievances and attacking each other's character during arguments instead of discussing the actual issue
Ignoring a deepening imbalance just because 'at least it's not that bad'

far.description: Far-distance Ankai is the mildest version of the Ankai combination. The attraction isn't as fierce as near-distance, and the power gap isn't as pronounced. What you feel between you is more of a subtle, push-and-pull tension — sparks fly when you get close, but you're each fine on your own. The distance gives both sides enough room to process each other's impact without getting overwhelmed by intense emotions like the near-distance version.
far.love: Far-distance Ankai romance is like a slow-paced film. Your attraction isn't the love-at-first-sight kind — it surfaces gradually through repeated contact. Because distance provides a buffer, you have more time to observe, evaluate, and decide whether to get closer. This is the most suitable distance for a serious Ankai relationship — you have enough space to stay clear-headed. The An side is less likely to over-invest at this distance, and the Kai side's influence is diluted to a manageable range. If you choose to be together, it's a deliberate decision rather than pure instinct.
far.career: Far-distance Ankai benefits from the distance buffering the power imbalance, allowing for more professional interactions. The An side builds systems and environments, while the Kai side steps in during critical moments to handle tough problems. This works best as a 'not daily collaboration, but mutual support at key moments' arrangement.
far.advice: The distance significantly lowers this relationship's risk factor. If you can make good use of the rational space that distance provides, this can be a relationship with tension but without losing control.
tips: Use the rational space that distance provides — check in with yourself each time you get closer
Don't deliberately close the distance to chase a stronger rush — moderation works best
Observe how they behave across different situations before making judgments
Enjoy the ebb-and-flow rhythm instead of being anxious about it
If something feels off, give yourself space rather than pushing through
avoid: Manufacturing opportunities to get closer just to chase intensity
Ignoring the occasional discomfort you feel — that's your intuition sending a signal
Taking the safety of distance as proof that 'this relationship has no issues'
Letting your guard down after the relationship stabilizes — the fundamental Ankai dynamic doesn't vanish with distance
Comparing your relationship to near-distance Ankai and feeling like yours isn't exciting enough

kisei.description: A complementary relationship where both sides' traits and abilities form contrasts. What you lack, they happen to have. What they need, you can provide. It takes time to adjust in the early stages, but once you get through the adaptation period, this becomes a relationship that drives real growth.
kisei.detailed: Kisei (危成) represents a relationship type that's complementary but requires adjustment. 'Ki' (危) hints at the challenges and risks of early interactions, while 'Sei' (成) points to the achievement and success that can ultimately be reached. Your personalities, thinking styles, and strengths often form contrasts — what you're good at, they might struggle with, and vice versa. These differences can cause friction and misunderstanding early on, requiring time to adapt to each other's ways. However, once you get past the adjustment period, these complementary traits become fuel for each other's growth. You learn from them what you were missing, and the relationship becomes richer and more complete as a result.
kisei.advice: Don't expect them to become like you. Learn to appreciate differences instead. Treat what sets you apart as a chance to learn and grow, and have patience through the early adjustment period.
tips: Learn to appreciate what makes you different from each other
Treat differences as opportunities for mutual learning
Be patient and invest time in getting through the early adaptation phase
During disagreements, remind yourself: their perspective fills in your blind spots
Take the initiative to learn about their area of expertise so the complementary advantage actually kicks in
avoid: Trying to change them to match your expectations
Giving up on communication because the differences make you uncomfortable
Rushing to conclude 'we're incompatible' during the adjustment phase
Mocking or belittling their different way of doing things
Seeing only the friction that differences cause while ignoring the growth and learning they bring
kisei.description_classic: (Editor's summary) Kisei: different yet complementary. 'Ki' is the difficulty of first meeting, 'Sei' is the achievement at the end. Uneasy at first acquaintance, close after long association. Through complementary virtues, great things can be accomplished.

near.description: Near-distance Kisei is the most collision-prone version of complementary relationships. Your differences are so stark that you might rub each other the wrong way on first meeting — their approach makes you frown, and your habits baffle them. But Sukuyodo pairs you together for a reason: what you're missing, they have. Those differences that bother you are actually the things you need to learn. Near distance means you interact frequently, which means more friction — but every friction point is a growth opportunity.
near.love: Near-distance Kisei romance is never boring. You probably argue more often than the average couple — not because you don't care, but because your thinking patterns and lifestyle habits are just that different. You like to plan before acting; they might wing it. You think the home should be tidy; they think 'creative chaos' is more comfortable. These differences will make you question your compatibility early on, but if you can push through the first six months of adjustment, you'll find they've taught you things you never would have learned on your own. The secret to success: swap 'my way is right' for 'let me try your way.'
near.career: In the workplace, the Ki side brings social connections and networking, while the Sei side brings professional depth. You're well-suited for joint training, coursework, or project completion, but less ideal for business trips or signing major contracts together. The initial adjustment cost is high, so establish a clear division of labor upfront.
near.advice: This is the relationship that makes you grow the fastest — but only if you're willing to embrace discomfort. Every point of friction is polishing you into a better version of yourself.
tips: Treat their different approach as a learning opportunity, not a flaw
When arguing, address the action, not the person — critique the method, not the character
Designate an 'adjustment period' and be extra tolerant of friction during that time
Find the few things you do have in common and build your connection from there
Periodically look back at what you've learned from each other — you'll be surprised by how much you've grown
avoid: Wanting to give up at the first sign of friction because you think you're incompatible
Trying to remake them into a copy of yourself
Dragging up old grievances or resorting to personal attacks during conflicts
Seeing only the problems that differences cause while missing the complementary value they bring
Blaming everything on 'we were never meant to work' instead of looking for solutions

mid.description: Mid-distance Kisei is the most balanced version of complementary relationships. Differences exist but won't overwhelm you, and the complementary effect is strong without requiring painful adjustment. Your personalities and working styles are noticeably different, but the differences fall within the range of 'I can actually appreciate that.' You find their approach interesting, and they think you have qualities they couldn't replicate.
mid.love: Mid-distance Kisei romance is like a moderately paced dance. Your steps are different, but when the music starts you find a pretty good groove together. Unlike near-distance where you're constantly stepping on toes, or far-distance where you can't find a shared rhythm, your differences bring more amusement than frustration. When they do something unexpected, your reaction is 'huh, that works too?' rather than 'why would you do that?' This just-right level of difference means you'll never run out of things to talk about.
mid.career: In the workplace, the Ki side's social skills plus the Sei side's professional depth creates strong synergy with low adjustment costs. You're well-suited to form a steady partnership: one expands the network, the other deepens the expertise. Each person's strengths neatly cover the other's weaknesses.
mid.advice: You're the kind of pair that respects each other more the longer you spend together. Give it time, and you'll become excellent teachers for each other.
tips: Appreciate their different approach — it's a free second perspective for you
Before making important decisions, get their take as a reference point
Share what you're good at and be open to learning what they're good at
Don't try to agree on everything — preserving differences is healthy
Have regular deep conversations to understand each other's thoughts and feelings
avoid: Dismissing their different methods as stupid or wrong
Only leveraging their strengths without offering support where they're weak
Making fun of their weaknesses in front of others
Stopping efforts to understand them deeper just because things are going okay
Letting small irritations from the adjustment period pile up until they become big problems

far.description: Far-distance Kisei is like two people from different worlds who happen to cross paths. Your differences might be visible in everything from fundamental values to daily habits — but precisely because the gap is so wide, neither of you expects the other to be like you. You knew from the start that they're different, so you're more likely to approach them with openness and curiosity rather than measuring them against your own standards.
far.love: Far-distance Kisei romance requires emotional maturity from both sides. Your differences aren't in the details — they're in lifestyle and worldview. This means the adjustment happens at a deeper level and demands more patience. But if you're both willing to step outside your comfort zones for each other, the growth this relationship brings is unmatched by any other. You'll become a person with broader horizons and greater capacity for acceptance. The prerequisite is that both of you treat 'different' as a gift rather than a burden.
far.career: Far-distance Kisei isn't ideal for close daily collaboration, but when you need to think outside the box, the Sei side's professional depth offers a fresh perspective, and the Ki side's network can open doors. Treat each other as external consultants — mutual support at critical junctures.
far.advice: The value of this relationship lies in expanding your world. You don't need to become like them — just become a more complete version of yourself because you know them.
tips: Approach their worldview with the curiosity of an anthropologist
Don't rush to judge this relationship — let time be the test
Find the shared values between you that remain constant despite all the differences
Learn to find a third path between completely different viewpoints
Accept that some differences will never disappear but can peacefully coexist
avoid: Judging their choices through the lens of your own values
Writing off the relationship from the start because the differences seem too vast
Focusing only on how they're 'not like me' while overlooking their inherent worth
Trying to be each other's renovation project
Choosing avoidance over facing and discussing differences when they arise
